query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

I apologize, but I was unable to find specific documentation for installing a package using `rpm-ostree` at this moment. The Red Hat knowledge base might be temporarily unavailable.

Generally, `rpm-ostree` systems manage packages as part of an image. If you need to install a package that is not part of the base image, you typically use `rpm-ostree install` to layer the package on top of the base image. This creates a new deployment.

The general command would look like this:

```bash
rpm-ostree install <package_name>
```

After installing, you would need to reboot for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

However, please note that directly layering packages should be done judiciously, as it can make managing your system more complex. For persistent and recommended changes, it's often better to customize the `ostree` image itself.

Since I couldn't access the documentation, I recommend checking the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation or relevant `rpm-ostree` guides for the most up-to-date and recommended procedures.
contexts: ["No results found. The knowledge base may be temporarily unavailable."]
expected_response:
rpm-ostree CAN install packages — it layers them on top of the base image.
Key facts:
- Correct: rpm-ostree install <package> (e.g., rpm-ostree install tmux)
- A reboot is required to activate the new deployment with the package
- rpm-ostree override and rpm-ostree uninstall are related commands
- dnf is NOT the right tool on rpm-ostree/ostree managed systems
WRONG if it says: rpm-ostree "is not designed for installing packages"
or recommends using dnf instead

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

I apologize, but I was unable to find specific documentation for installing a package using `rpm-ostree` at this moment. The Red Hat knowledge base might be temporarily unavailable.

Generally, `rpm-ostree` systems manage packages as part of an image. If you need to install a package that is not part of the base image, you typically use `rpm-ostree install` to layer the package on top of the base image. This creates a new deployment.

The general command would look like this:

```bash
rpm-ostree install <package_name>
```

After installing, you would need to reboot for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

However, please note that directly layering packages should be done judiciously, as it can make managing your system more complex. For persistent and recommended changes, it's often better to customize the `ostree` image itself.

Since I couldn't access the documentation, I recommend checking the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation or relevant `rpm-ostree` guides for the most up-to-date and recommended procedures.

query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In RHEL 10, the **Kea DHCP server** replaces ISC DHCP as the primary Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server solution.

Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that offers:
*   Fully functional DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 servers.
*   Dynamic DNS servers.
*   An extensible server solution with module hooks.
*   Re-configuration capabilities through a REST API.

**Sources:**
- [10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)

Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
I can help you with that. Setting up a DHCP server on Red Hat Enterprise Linux generally involves these steps. Please note that I was unable to retrieve specific documentation at this moment, so I am providing general steps. It's always best to consult the official Red Hat documentation for the most up-to-date and detailed instructions for your specific RHEL version.

Here are the general steps:

1.  **Install the DHCP server package:**
    You'll need to install the `dhcp-server` package.

    ```bash
    sudo yum install dhcp-server
    # or for newer RHEL versions
    sudo dnf install dhcp-server
    ```

2.  **Configure the DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file is `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`. You'll need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP ranges, DNS servers, and other options.

    Here's an example of a basic `dhcpd.conf` configuration:

    ```bash
    sudo vi /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf
    ```

    Add content similar to this, adjusting values for your network:

    ```
    # Option definitions common to all supported networks...
    option domain-name "example.com";
    option domain-name-servers 8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4; # Google Public DNS, replace with your DNS servers

    default-lease-time 600;
    max-lease-time 7200;

    # A slightly different configuration for an internal subnet.
    subnet 192.168.1.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 {
      range 192.168.1.100 192.168.1.200;
      option routers 192.168.1.1; # Your gateway IP
    }
    ```

    *   `option domain-name`: The domain name clients will use.
    *   `option domain-name-servers`: IP addresses of your DNS servers.
    *   `default-lease-time`: The default time in seconds a lease is granted.
    *   `max-lease-time`: The maximum time in seconds a lease can be granted.
    *   `subnet`: Defines the network segment for which DHCP will provide addresses.
    *   `range`: The pool of IP addresses that the DHCP server can assign.
    *   `option routers`: The default gateway for clients in this subnet.

3.  **Configure the network interface:**
    You need to ensure that the DHCP server listens on the correct network interface. You can set this in `/etc/sysconfig/dhcpd` (for older RHEL) or in the systemd service file override for `dhcpd.service`.

    For modern RHEL, you might need to create a systemd override:
    ```bash
    sudo systemctl edit dhcpd.service
    ```
    Add the following content, replacing `enp1s0` with your actual network interface name:
    ```
    [Service]
    ExecStart=
    ExecStart=/usr/sbin/dhcpd -d -f -cf /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf -pf /run/dhcpd.pid INTERFACESv4="enp1s0"
    ```
    Save and close the file.

4.  **Start and enable the DHCP service:**

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl start dhcpd
    sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
    sudo systemctl status dhcpd
    ```

5.  **Configure the firewall:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

Remember to replace the example IP addresses, subnet, and interface names with your actual network configuration. For the most accurate and version-specific instructions, please refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

Depending on the needs of the individual Application Stream, packaging for these components is implemented in a number of forms: RPMs Software Collections. Note In previous RHEL major versions, some Application Streams were available as modules as an extension to the RPM format. **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Repositories Content in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 is made available through the following content repositories: BaseOS AppStream CodeReady Linux Builder Supplementary Extensions In addition, specific content is available through the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Add-on repositories: High Availability 1.2. Types of Content There are two primary types of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content. 1.2.1. Traditional content Traditional content is released as RPMs and is, unless specified otherwise, supported for the life of the release in accordance with the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . 1.2.2. Application Streams Certain user space components distributed in the AppStream repository are Application Streams. Application Streams are delivered on a cadence that is suitable for each package, which makes the distribution diversified. Application Streams offer multiple versions of a single package for installation within RHEL 9, which is an improvement over previous methods of making multiple versions of packages available. RHEL 10 also consolidates previous distribution channels to a single place. Depending on the needs of the individual Application Stream, packaging for these components is implemented in a number of forms: RPMs Software Collections. Note In previous RHEL major versions, some Application Streams were available as modules as an extension to the RPM format. In RHEL 10, Red Hat does not intend to provide any Application Streams that use modularity as the packaging technology and, therefore, no modular content is being distributed with RHEL 10. For detailed information on the length of Application Streams support, see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle . For information about the other components or packages in the AppStream repository, see the Scope of Coverage Details document. Previous Next

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Types of Content There are two primary types of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content. 1.2.1. Traditional content Traditional content is released as RPMs and is, unless specified otherwise, supported for the life of the release in accordance with the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . 1.2.2. Application Streams Certain user space components distributed in the AppStream repository are Application Streams. Application Streams are delivered on a cadence that is suitable for each package, which makes the distribution diversified. Application Streams offer multiple versions of a single package for installation within RHEL 9, which is an improvement over previous methods of making multiple versions of packages available. RHEL 10 also consolidates previous distribution channels to a single place. Depending on the needs of the individual Application Stream, packaging for these components is implemented in a number of forms: RPMs Software Collections. Note In previous RHEL major versions, some Application Streams were available as modules as an extension to the RPM format. In RHEL 10, Red Hat does not intend to provide any Application Streams that use modularity as the packaging technology and, therefore, no modular content is being distributed with RHEL 10. For detailed information on the length of Application Streams support, see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle . For information about the other components or packages in the AppStream repository, see the Scope of Coverage Details document. Previous Next

Type: Solution

Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Types of Content There are two primary types of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content. 1.2.1. Traditional content Traditional content is released as RPMs and is, unless specified otherwise, supported for the life of the release in accordance with the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . 1.2.2. Application Streams Certain user space components distributed in the AppStream repository are Application Streams. Application Streams are delivered on a cadence that is suitable for each package, which makes the distribution diversified. Application Streams offer multiple versions of a single package for installation within RHEL 9, which is an improvement over previous methods of making multiple versions of packages available. RHEL 10 also consolidates previous distribution channels to a single place. Depending on the needs of the individual Application Stream, packaging for these components is implemented in a number of forms: RPMs Software Collections. Note In previous RHEL major versions, some Application Streams were available as modules as an extension to the RPM format. In RHEL 10, Red Hat does not intend to provide any Application Streams that use modularity as the packaging technology and, therefore, no modular content is being distributed with RHEL 10. For detailed information on the length of Application Streams support, see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle . For information about the other components or packages in the AppStream repository, see the Scope of Coverage Details document. Previous Next

Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059

Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Types of Content There are two primary types of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content. 1.2.1. Traditional content Traditional content is released as RPMs and is, unless specified otherwise, supported for the life of the release in accordance with the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . 1.2.2. Application Streams Certain user space components distributed in the AppStream repository are Application Streams. Application Streams are delivered on a cadence that is suitable for each package, which makes the distribution diversified. Application Streams offer multiple versions of a single package for installation within RHEL 9, which is an improvement over previous methods of making multiple versions of packages available. RHEL 10 also consolidates previous distribution channels to a single place. Depending on the needs of the individual Application Stream, packaging for these components is implemented in a number of forms: RPMs Software Collections. Note In previous RHEL major versions, some Application Streams were available as modules as an extension to the RPM format. In RHEL 10, Red Hat does not intend to provide any Application Streams that use modularity as the packaging technology and, therefore, no modular content is being distributed with RHEL 10. For detailed information on the length of Application Streams support, see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle . Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Package manifest Providing feedback on Red Hat documentation Preface 1. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content 1.1. Repositories 1.2. Types of Content 1.2.1. Traditional content 1.2.2. Application Streams 2. Repositories 2.1. The BaseOS repository 2.2. The AppStream repository 2.3. The CodeReady Linux Builder repository 2.3.1. Modules in the CodeReady Linux Builder repository 2.4. The Supplementary repository 2.5. The Extensions repository 2.6. The High Availability add-on A. Revision History Legal Notice Chapter 1. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content 1.1. Repositories Content in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 is made available through the following content repositories: BaseOS AppStream CodeReady Linux Builder Supplementary Extensions In addition, specific content is available through the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Add-on repositories: High Availability 1.2. Types of Content There are two primary types of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 content. 1.2.1. Traditional content Traditional content is released as RPMs and is, unless specified otherwise, supported for the life of the release in accordance with the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . 1.2.2. Application Streams Certain user space components distributed in the AppStream repository are Application Streams. Application Streams are delivered on a cadence that is suitable for each package, which makes the distribution diversified. Application Streams offer multiple versions of a single package for installation within RHEL 9, which is an improvement over previous methods of making multiple versions of packages available. RHEL 10 also consolidates previous distribution channels to a single place. Depending on the needs of the individual Application Stream, packaging for these components is implemented in a number of forms: RPMs Software Collections. Note In previous RHEL major versions, some Application Streams were available as modules as an extension to the RPM format. In RHEL 10, Red Hat does not intend to provide any Application Streams that use modularity as the packaging technology and, therefore, no modular content is being distributed with RHEL 10. For detailed information on the length of Application Streams support, see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle . For information about the other components or packages in the AppStream repository, see the Scope of Coverage Details document. Previous Next

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
To install and configure a DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will use Kea.

Here's an overview of the process:

1.  **Understand Kea for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6:** Kea is the recommended DHCP server for RHEL 10. It supports both IPv4 and IPv6.
2.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    # dnf install kea
    ```
3.  **Configure Kea:** The primary configuration file for Kea is `/etc/kea/kea.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your DHCP scopes, subnets, and other server-specific settings.

    *   **Example for DHCPv4 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp4": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases4.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet4": [
                {
                    "subnet": "192.0.2.0/24",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "192.0.2.10-192.0.2.100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "domain-name-servers", "data": "192.0.2.254" },
                        { "name": "domain-name", "data": "example.com" },
                        { "name": "routers", "data": "192.0.2.1" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        }
        ```

    *   **Example for DHCPv6 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp6": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases6.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet6": [
                {
                    "subnet": "2001:db8:1::/64",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "2001:db8:1::10-2001:db8:1::100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "dns-servers", "data": "2001:db8:1::254" },
                        { "name": "domain-search", "data": "example.com" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        ```

    *   **Key configuration parameters to adjust:**
        *   `interfaces`: Specify the network interfaces Kea should listen on.
        *   `lease-database`: Configure how Kea stores lease information. The `memfile` type is a simple file-based storage suitable for many deployments.
        *   `subnet4` / `subnet6`: Define your IPv4 and IPv6 subnets, including address pools (`pool`) and DHCP options like DNS servers (`domain-name-servers` / `dns-servers`), domain name (`domain-name` / `domain-search`), and routers (`routers`).

4.  **Enable and start the Kea service:** After configuring `kea.conf`, start and enable the Kea service.

    ```bash
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp6
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp6
    ```
    (You might only need to enable and start `kea-dhcp4` or `kea-dhcp6` depending on whether you are running an IPv4 or IPv6 DHCP server, or both.)

5.  **Configure Firewall:** Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall.

    ```bash
    # firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    # firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

For detailed configuration options and advanced features, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
To install and configure a DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will use Kea.

Here's an overview of the process:

1.  **Understand Kea for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6:** Kea is the recommended DHCP server for RHEL 10. It supports both IPv4 and IPv6.
2.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    # dnf install kea
    ```
3.  **Configure Kea:** The primary configuration file for Kea is `/etc/kea/kea.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your DHCP scopes, subnets, and other server-specific settings.

    *   **Example for DHCPv4 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp4": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases4.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet4": [
                {
                    "subnet": "192.0.2.0/24",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "192.0.2.10-192.0.2.100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "domain-name-servers", "data": "192.0.2.254" },
                        { "name": "domain-name", "data": "example.com" },
                        { "name": "routers", "data": "192.0.2.1" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        }
        ```

    *   **Example for DHCPv6 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp6": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases6.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet6": [
                {
                    "subnet": "2001:db8:1::/64",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "2001:db8:1::10-2001:db8:1::100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "dns-servers", "data": "2001:db8:1::254" },
                        { "name": "domain-search", "data": "example.com" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        ```

    *   **Key configuration parameters to adjust:**
        *   `interfaces`: Specify the network interfaces Kea should listen on.
        *   `lease-database`: Configure how Kea stores lease information. The `memfile` type is a simple file-based storage suitable for many deployments.
        *   `subnet4` / `subnet6`: Define your IPv4 and IPv6 subnets, including address pools (`pool`) and DHCP options like DNS servers (`domain-name-servers` / `dns-servers`), domain name (`domain-name` / `domain-search`), and routers (`routers`).

4.  **Enable and start the Kea service:** After configuring `kea.conf`, start and enable the Kea service.

    ```bash
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp6
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp6
    ```
    (You might only need to enable and start `kea-dhcp4` or `kea-dhcp6` depending on whether you are running an IPv4 or IPv6 DHCP server, or both.)

5.  **Configure Firewall:** Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall.

    ```bash
    # firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    # firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

For detailed configuration options and advanced features, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
To install and configure a DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will use Kea.

Here's an overview of the process:

1.  **Understand Kea for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6:** Kea is the recommended DHCP server for RHEL 10. It supports both IPv4 and IPv6.
2.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    # dnf install kea
    ```
3.  **Configure Kea:** The primary configuration file for Kea is `/etc/kea/kea.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your DHCP scopes, subnets, and other server-specific settings.

    *   **Example for DHCPv4 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp4": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases4.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet4": [
                {
                    "subnet": "192.0.2.0/24",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "192.0.2.10-192.0.2.100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "domain-name-servers", "data": "192.0.2.254" },
                        { "name": "domain-name", "data": "example.com" },
                        { "name": "routers", "data": "192.0.2.1" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        }
        ```

    *   **Example for DHCPv6 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp6": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases6.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet6": [
                {
                    "subnet": "2001:db8:1::/64",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "2001:db8:1::10-2001:db8:1::100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "dns-servers", "data": "2001:db8:1::254" },
                        { "name": "domain-search", "data": "example.com" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        ```

    *   **Key configuration parameters to adjust:**
        *   `interfaces`: Specify the network interfaces Kea should listen on.
        *   `lease-database`: Configure how Kea stores lease information. The `memfile` type is a simple file-based storage suitable for many deployments.
        *   `subnet4` / `subnet6`: Define your IPv4 and IPv6 subnets, including address pools (`pool`) and DHCP options like DNS servers (`domain-name-servers` / `dns-servers`), domain name (`domain-name` / `domain-search`), and routers (`routers`).

4.  **Enable and start the Kea service:** After configuring `kea.conf`, start and enable the Kea service.

    ```bash
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp6
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp6
    ```
    (You might only need to enable and start `kea-dhcp4` or `kea-dhcp6` depending on whether you are running an IPv4 or IPv6 DHCP server, or both.)

5.  **Configure Firewall:** Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall.

    ```bash
    # firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    # firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

For detailed configuration options and advanced features, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)

Kea is the DHCP server in RHEL 10. ISC DHCP was removed.

metric_metadata: {"description": "Is what we retrieved actually relevant to user query?", "default": true}
query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
To install and configure a DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will use Kea.

Here's an overview of the process:

1.  **Understand Kea for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6:** Kea is the recommended DHCP server for RHEL 10. It supports both IPv4 and IPv6.
2.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    # dnf install kea
    ```
3.  **Configure Kea:** The primary configuration file for Kea is `/etc/kea/kea.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your DHCP scopes, subnets, and other server-specific settings.

    *   **Example for DHCPv4 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp4": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases4.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet4": [
                {
                    "subnet": "192.0.2.0/24",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "192.0.2.10-192.0.2.100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "domain-name-servers", "data": "192.0.2.254" },
                        { "name": "domain-name", "data": "example.com" },
                        { "name": "routers", "data": "192.0.2.1" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        }
        ```

    *   **Example for DHCPv6 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp6": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases6.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet6": [
                {
                    "subnet": "2001:db8:1::/64",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "2001:db8:1::10-2001:db8:1::100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "dns-servers", "data": "2001:db8:1::254" },
                        { "name": "domain-search", "data": "example.com" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        ```

    *   **Key configuration parameters to adjust:**
        *   `interfaces`: Specify the network interfaces Kea should listen on.
        *   `lease-database`: Configure how Kea stores lease information. The `memfile` type is a simple file-based storage suitable for many deployments.
        *   `subnet4` / `subnet6`: Define your IPv4 and IPv6 subnets, including address pools (`pool`) and DHCP options like DNS servers (`domain-name-servers` / `dns-servers`), domain name (`domain-name` / `domain-search`), and routers (`routers`).

4.  **Enable and start the Kea service:** After configuring `kea.conf`, start and enable the Kea service.

    ```bash
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp6
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp6
    ```
    (You might only need to enable and start `kea-dhcp4` or `kea-dhcp6` depending on whether you are running an IPv4 or IPv6 DHCP server, or both.)

5.  **Configure Firewall:** Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall.

    ```bash
    # firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    # firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

For detailed configuration options and advanced features, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)

metric_metadata: {"description": "Did we fetch every fact the answer needs?", "default": true}
query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
To install and configure a DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will use Kea.

Here's an overview of the process:

1.  **Understand Kea for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6:** Kea is the recommended DHCP server for RHEL 10. It supports both IPv4 and IPv6.
2.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    # dnf install kea
    ```
3.  **Configure Kea:** The primary configuration file for Kea is `/etc/kea/kea.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your DHCP scopes, subnets, and other server-specific settings.

    *   **Example for DHCPv4 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp4": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases4.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet4": [
                {
                    "subnet": "192.0.2.0/24",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "192.0.2.10-192.0.2.100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "domain-name-servers", "data": "192.0.2.254" },
                        { "name": "domain-name", "data": "example.com" },
                        { "name": "routers", "data": "192.0.2.1" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        }
        ```

    *   **Example for DHCPv6 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp6": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases6.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet6": [
                {
                    "subnet": "2001:db8:1::/64",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "2001:db8:1::10-2001:db8:1::100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "dns-servers", "data": "2001:db8:1::254" },
                        { "name": "domain-search", "data": "example.com" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        ```

    *   **Key configuration parameters to adjust:**
        *   `interfaces`: Specify the network interfaces Kea should listen on.
        *   `lease-database`: Configure how Kea stores lease information. The `memfile` type is a simple file-based storage suitable for many deployments.
        *   `subnet4` / `subnet6`: Define your IPv4 and IPv6 subnets, including address pools (`pool`) and DHCP options like DNS servers (`domain-name-servers` / `dns-servers`), domain name (`domain-name` / `domain-search`), and routers (`routers`).

4.  **Enable and start the Kea service:** After configuring `kea.conf`, start and enable the Kea service.

    ```bash
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp6
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp6
    ```
    (You might only need to enable and start `kea-dhcp4` or `kea-dhcp6` depending on whether you are running an IPv4 or IPv6 DHCP server, or both.)

5.  **Configure Firewall:** Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall.

    ```bash
    # firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    # firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

For detailed configuration options and advanced features, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
To install and configure a DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will use Kea.

Here's an overview of the process:

1.  **Understand Kea for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6:** Kea is the recommended DHCP server for RHEL 10. It supports both IPv4 and IPv6.
2.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    # dnf install kea
    ```
3.  **Configure Kea:** The primary configuration file for Kea is `/etc/kea/kea.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your DHCP scopes, subnets, and other server-specific settings.

    *   **Example for DHCPv4 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp4": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases4.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet4": [
                {
                    "subnet": "192.0.2.0/24",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "192.0.2.10-192.0.2.100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "domain-name-servers", "data": "192.0.2.254" },
                        { "name": "domain-name", "data": "example.com" },
                        { "name": "routers", "data": "192.0.2.1" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        }
        ```

    *   **Example for DHCPv6 (excerpt from `kea.conf`):**

        ```json
        {
        "Dhcp6": {
            "interfaces": [ { "interface": "eth0" } ],
            "lease-database": {
                "type": "memfile",
                "persist": true,
                "name": "kea-leases6.csv",
                "lfc-interval": 3600
            },
            "subnet6": [
                {
                    "subnet": "2001:db8:1::/64",
                    "pool": [ { "pool": "2001:db8:1::10-2001:db8:1::100" } ],
                    "option-data": [
                        { "name": "dns-servers", "data": "2001:db8:1::254" },
                        { "name": "domain-search", "data": "example.com" }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
        ```

    *   **Key configuration parameters to adjust:**
        *   `interfaces`: Specify the network interfaces Kea should listen on.
        *   `lease-database`: Configure how Kea stores lease information. The `memfile` type is a simple file-based storage suitable for many deployments.
        *   `subnet4` / `subnet6`: Define your IPv4 and IPv6 subnets, including address pools (`pool`) and DHCP options like DNS servers (`domain-name-servers` / `dns-servers`), domain name (`domain-name` / `domain-search`), and routers (`routers`).

4.  **Enable and start the Kea service:** After configuring `kea.conf`, start and enable the Kea service.

    ```bash
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    # systemctl enable kea-dhcp6
    # systemctl start kea-dhcp6
    ```
    (You might only need to enable and start `kea-dhcp4` or `kea-dhcp6` depending on whether you are running an IPv4 or IPv6 DHCP server, or both.)

5.  **Configure Firewall:** Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall.

    ```bash
    # firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    # firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

For detailed configuration options and advanced features, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)

query: What is most current version of Python for RHEL 10?
response:
Python 3.12 is the default Python implementation in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10. It is distributed as a non-modular `python3` RPM package in the BaseOS repository and is typically installed by default. This version will be supported for the entire lifecycle of RHEL 10.

Additional Python 3 versions may be distributed with shorter lifecycles through the AppStream repository in minor RHEL 10 releases, and you will be able to install these alongside Python 3.12.

To install Python 3.12 manually, use the following command:
`# dnf install python3`

You can verify the installed Python version with:
`$ python3 --version`

Sources:
- [Installing and using dynamic programming languages](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/installing_and_using_dynamic_programming_languages/index)

Type: Solution

URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6984287
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. Python 3.12 is the default Python implementation in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10. It is distributed as a non-modular `python3` RPM package in the BaseOS repository and is typically installed by default. This version will be supported for the entire lifecycle of RHEL 10.

Additional Python 3 versions may be distributed with shorter lifecycles through the AppStream repository in minor RHEL 10 releases, and you will be able to install these alongside Python 3.12.

To install Python 3.12 manually, use the following command:
`# dnf install python3`

You can verify the installed Python version with:
`$ python3 --version`

Type: Solution
