URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6984287
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. Python 3.12 is the default Python implementation in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10. It is distributed as a non-modular `python3` RPM package in the BaseOS repository and is typically installed by default. This version will be supported for the entire lifecycle of RHEL 10.

Additional Python 3 versions may be distributed with shorter lifecycles through the AppStream repository in minor RHEL 10 releases, and you will be able to install these alongside Python 3.12.

To install Python 3.12 manually, use the following command:
`# dnf install python3`

You can verify the installed Python version with:
`$ python3 --version`

Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. Python 3.12 is the default Python implementation in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10. It is distributed as a non-modular `python3` RPM package in the BaseOS repository and is typically installed by default. This version will be supported for the entire lifecycle of RHEL 10.

Additional Python 3 versions may be distributed with shorter lifecycles through the AppStream repository in minor RHEL 10 releases, and you will be able to install these alongside Python 3.12.

To install Python 3.12 manually, use the following command:
`# dnf install python3`

You can verify the installed Python version with:
`$ python3 --version`

Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? response:
Python 3.12 is the default Python implementation in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10. It is distributed as a non-modular `python3` RPM package in the BaseOS repository and is typically installed by default. This version will be supported for the entire lifecycle of RHEL 10.

Additional Python 3 versions may be distributed with shorter lifecycles through the AppStream repository in minor RHEL 10 releases, and you will be able to install these alongside Python 3.12.

To install Python 3.12 manually, use the following command:
`# dnf install python3`

You can verify the installed Python version with:
`$ python3 --version`

Python software intended for distribution is often byte-compiled, but not in the way described in this document. The described procedure aims not to conform to the community standards, but to be simple. For real-world Python guidelines, see Content from docs.python.org is not included. Software Packaging and Distribution . You can also raw-interpret Python source code. However, the byte-compiled version is faster. Therefore, RPM packagers prefer to package the byte-compiled version for distribution to end users. Raw-interpreting Software written in shell scripting languages, such as Bash, is always executed by raw-interpreting. 4.3.2.1.Â Byte-compiling a sample Python program Byte-compile a sample Python source code to improve software performance. Python code can also be raw-interpreted, but byte-compiled code runs faster. By choosing byte-compiling over raw-interpreting of Python source code, you can create faster software. A sample Hello World program written in the Python programming language ( pello.py ) has the following contents: print("Hello World") Procedure Byte-compile the pello.py file: $ python -m compileall pello.py Verify that a byte-compiled version of the file is created: $ ls pass:[ pycache ] pello.cpython-311.pyc Note that the package version in the output might differ depending on which Python version is installed. Python 3.12 is the default Python implementation in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10. It is distributed as a non-modular `python3` RPM package in the BaseOS repository and is typically installed by default. This version will be supported for the entire lifecycle of RHEL 10.

Additional Python 3 versions may be distributed with shorter lifecycles through the AppStream repository in minor RHEL 10 releases, and you will be able to install these alongside Python 3.12.

To install Python 3.12 manually, use the following command:
`# dnf install python3`

You can verify the installed Python version with:
`$ python3 --version`

Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 involves a general process of configuration transfer and validation, as there isn't a single, specific migration tool provided for this service between major RHEL versions. Here are the general steps you would follow:

1.  **Backup Existing Configuration (RHEL 9):**
    *   Identify and back up all DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   If you have custom scripts or any other related files, ensure they are also backed up.
    *   It's also good practice to back up the `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases` file, which contains current lease information.

2.  **Install DHCP Server (RHEL 10):**
    *   On your new RHEL 10 system, install the DHCP server package:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

3.  **Transfer and Adapt Configuration (RHEL 10):**
    *   Copy your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` file to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` on the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Review the copied configuration file. While `dhcpd` configurations are generally stable across RHEL major versions, there might be minor syntax changes, deprecated options, or new features. Consult the `man dhcpd.conf` page on RHEL 10 for any version-specific differences.
    *   If you backed up `dhcpd.leases`, you can copy it to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`. Ensure proper permissions and ownership.

4.  **Configure Firewall (RHEL 10):**
    *   Allow DHCP traffic through the firewall on the RHEL 10 system:
        ```bash
        sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
        sudo firewall-cmd --reload
        ```

5.  **Enable and Start DHCP Service (RHEL 10):**
    *   Enable and start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Check the service status and logs (`journalctl -u dhcpd`) for any errors.

6.  **Testing:**
    *   Thoroughly test the new DHCP server by bringing up client machines and verifying that they receive IP addresses and other DHCP options correctly.
    *   If you have static IP assignments or reservations, ensure they are also working as expected.
    *   Monitor the `dhcpd.leases` file for new entries.

7.  **Disable Old DHCP Server (RHEL 9):**
    *   Once you have confirmed that the RHEL 10 DHCP server is functioning correctly, disable the DHCP service on the RHEL 9 system to prevent conflicts:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl stop dhcpd
        sudo systemctl disable dhcpd
        ```

Always consult the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL 9 and RHEL 10 versions for any service-specific changes or recommendations.
